citation: Bergstein VE, Lu AZ, Hodgens BH, Bodendorfer BM, DeFroda SF, Kaplan J, Aiyer AA. Fantasy football points capture performance declines in National Football League offensive skill players following an ankle injury. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2024.
abstract: This retrospective matched cohort study investigated performance changes in NFL offensive skill players following ankle injuries using fantasy football points as a validated performance metric. Researchers analyzed 303 NFL players from 2009 to 2020 using Pro Football Reference and FantasyData. Players with ankle injuries were matched to uninjured controls. Fantasy points per game (PPG) and total fantasy points were compared pre- and post-injury. Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) experienced statistically significant declines in fantasy PPG and total points post-injury (p<0.0001), while quarterbacks showed no significant performance change. The study supports the use of fantasy football statistics as an objective, position-appropriate measure of performance decline following ankle injuries in the NFL.
tldr: Ankle injuries significantly reduce fantasy football performance in route-running skill players (RBs, WRs, TEs) but not QBs, suggesting position-specific functional demands determine the extent of performance decline following ankle injury.
methods: Retrospective matched cohort study; N=303 NFL players (2009–2020); data from Pro Football Reference and FantasyData; matched injured players to uninjured controls by position, age, and experience; compared fantasy PPG and total points pre- vs. post-injury; statistical analysis with paired comparisons (p<0.0001 threshold).
findings: Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) showed statistically significant declines in both fantasy PPG and total fantasy points following ankle injury (p<0.0001). QBs showed no significant performance change post-ankle injury. Fantasy football statistics served as a valid proxy for on-field performance decline in skill positions.
limitations: Fantasy points may not capture all aspects of athletic performance; sample limited to NFL players from 2009–2020; matching variables may not fully control for all confounders; severity and type of ankle injury not uniformly classified; return-to-play criteria not standardized across teams.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should expect meaningful performance decrements in route-running positions (RBs, WRs, TEs) following ankle injuries. Fantasy football point metrics can supplement traditional performance monitoring tools to quantify post-injury functional decline. Return-to-play criteria for these positions should account for the high ankle mobility and agility demands intrinsic to their roles.
athleteDev: For developing NFL skill players, ankle injury prevention and targeted rehabilitation protocols are critical given the significant performance impact documented in route runners. Position-specific functional testing after ankle injury should be incorporated before return to full competition.
rtp: Route runners (RBs, WRs, TEs) experience significant performance declines post-ankle injury; return-to-play decisions should incorporate objective performance metrics (e.g., fantasy PPG proxies) in addition to clinical clearance. QBs appear less affected and may return to performance baseline sooner after ankle injury.

citation: Cosio PL, Moreno-Simonet L, Fernández D, Lloret M, Padulles X, Padulles JM, Farran-Codina A, Rodas G, Cadefau JA. Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. European Journal of Applied Physiology. 2024.
abstract: This study examined whether early rate of torque development (RTD) is a more sensitive marker of residual hamstring fatigue after soccer match play than maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) torque. Nineteen male regional first-division soccer players underwent repeated measures assessments at pre-match (MD-1), post-match (MD+0), MD+1, MD+2, and MD+3. Early RTD (RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 ms) remained significantly reduced at 72 hours post-match (MD+3), while MVIC torque had recovered by that time point. Biomarkers including creatine kinase (CK), sarcomeric mitochondrial creatine kinase (sMtCK), malondialdehyde (MDA), and irisin remained elevated throughout the monitoring period. These findings indicate that early RTD captures residual neuromuscular fatigue that persists beyond when maximal strength has recovered, making it a more sensitive tool for post-match recovery monitoring.
tldr: Early rate of torque development (RTD0–50 and RTD0–100) remains depressed 72 hours after a soccer match even after MVIC torque has recovered, suggesting RTD is a more sensitive marker of residual hamstring fatigue and should be incorporated into post-match recovery monitoring.
methods: Repeated measures design; N=19 male regional first-division soccer players; assessment time points: MD-1, MD+0, MD+1, MD+2, MD+3; measures included MVIC torque, early RTD (RTD0–50, RTD0–100), and blood biomarkers (CK, sMtCK, MDA, irisin); isokinetic dynamometry used for torque and RTD measurements.
findings: RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 remained significantly reduced at MD+3 (72h post-match) while MVIC torque had recovered. CK, sMtCK, MDA, and irisin remained elevated throughout MD+0 to MD+3. Early RTD was more sensitive to residual hamstring fatigue than maximal isometric strength, indicating incomplete neuromuscular recovery despite normalized MVIC.
limitations: Small sample size (n=19); limited to male regional first-division players, reducing generalizability to elite or female populations; single match observation limits extrapolation to congested fixture periods; no control for training load between match and assessment days.
practicalImplications: Standard MVIC-based strength testing may give a false sense of recovery readiness after a soccer match. Early RTD assessment (particularly RTD0–50 and RTD0–100) should be incorporated into post-match monitoring protocols as it reveals persistent neuromuscular deficits up to 72 hours post-match. This has implications for scheduling training intensity and contact sessions during the recovery microcycle.
athleteDev: Developing soccer and football players should be monitored using RTD-based assessments post-match, not solely maximal strength tests, to accurately gauge neuromuscular readiness. Coaches and S&C staff should avoid high-intensity sprint or power sessions within 72 hours of competition based on these recovery timelines.
rtp: Following hamstring strain or fatigue in football players, RTD0–50 and RTD0–100 are more sensitive return-to-play markers than MVIC torque alone. Practitioners should not rely solely on normalized maximal isometric strength as clearance criteria; early RTD should be within normal ranges before progression to full practice or competition.

citation: de Oliveira DM, Silva AKAC, Macedo AG, Fernandes MB, Fernandes EV. Global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the last 20 years (2004-2024). Sports. 2025;13:365.
abstract: This scientometric review analyzed global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the past 20 years (2004–2024). A total of 526 original articles were retrieved from Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus databases. Bibliometric analysis was performed using Bibliometrix and VOSviewer. Publications increased by 1,450% over the study period. The United Kingdom led production (46.6% of articles). Nutrients was the most productive journal. Morton J and Maughan R were identified as the most productive authors. Early research trends focused on hydration and carbohydrate supplementation, while recent trends have shifted toward low energy availability (LEA), polyphenols, and recovery nutrition. The findings highlight the rapid growth of football-specific sports nutrition research and identify emerging areas for future investigation.
tldr: A 20-year scientometric analysis reveals a 1,450% increase in sports nutrition and football research, with UK institutions leading, and a thematic shift from hydration/carbohydrate focus toward low energy availability, polyphenols, and recovery strategies.
methods: Scientometric review; 526 original articles from Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus (2004–2024); bibliometric analysis using Bibliometrix R package and VOSviewer; keyword co-occurrence and co-citation analysis; no experimental data collection.
findings: 1,450% increase in publications from 2004 to 2024; UK led research output (46.6%); Nutrients was the top journal; Morton J and Maughan R were the most productive authors; early research themes = hydration and carbohydrates; recent themes = LEA, polyphenols, and recovery nutrition.
limitations: Scientometric design cannot assess research quality or clinical applicability; limited to three databases (WoS, PubMed, Scopus), potentially missing relevant publications in other sources; keyword selection may introduce selection bias; restricted to English-language publications.
practicalImplications: Sports nutrition practitioners working with football players should be aware of emerging research priorities: low energy availability, polyphenol supplementation, and recovery nutrition are increasingly evidence-based areas. Traditional foci on hydration and carbohydrates remain important but are well-established. Staff should stay current with Nutrients journal and leading researchers (Morton, Maughan) for cutting-edge guidance.
athleteDev: Football athlete development programs should increasingly incorporate LEA screening, polyphenol-rich dietary strategies, and structured recovery nutrition protocols based on the direction of current research. Nutrition periodization aligned with training phases is an emerging priority.
rtp: Recovery nutrition, including polyphenol supplementation and optimized carbohydrate-protein timing, is an emerging focus in football nutrition research. Return-to-play nutrition protocols should integrate these evidence-based strategies to accelerate tissue repair and reduce systemic inflammation.

citation: Hamstring injuries are increasing in men's professional football: every cloud has a silver lining.
Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the National Football League: An epidemiological study. Journal of Orthopaedics. 2022.
abstract: This epidemiological study examined the incidence and distribution of hamstring injuries (HSIs) across NFL positions over four regular seasons (2013–2016). Researchers analyzed 78 hamstring injuries using publicly available NFL gamebooks and Foxsports.com data. Standardized incidence ratios (SIRs) were calculated by position. Linebackers (SIR 2.02) and defensive backs (SIR 1.62) demonstrated the highest incidence of HSIs, while offensive linemen (SIR 0.28) and defensive linemen (SIR 0.29) showed the lowest. Playing surface and short rest intervals did not significantly affect HSI rates (p=0.082 and p=0.959, respectively). The mean week of HSI occurrence was 8.05 (median 7.5), suggesting mid-season accumulation. These findings support position-specific injury prevention strategies targeting LBs and DBs.
tldr: NFL linebackers and defensive backs have the highest hamstring injury rates, while linemen have the lowest. Playing surface and short rest did not significantly influence injury rates, and injuries tend to cluster mid-season around week 8.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological study; N=78 HSIs across 2013–2016 NFL regular seasons; data from publicly available NFL gamebooks and Foxsports.com; standardized incidence ratios (SIRs) calculated by position; chi-square and Fisher's exact tests for surface and rest interval analyses.
findings: LBs had highest HSI incidence (SIR 2.02), followed by DBs (SIR 1.62). OL (SIR 0.28) and DL (SIR 0.29) had lowest incidence. Playing surface (p=0.082) and short rest (p=0.959) were not significant predictors. Mean week of injury = 8.05 (median 7.5), indicating mid-season clustering.
limitations: Retrospective design relying on public data sources may undercount injuries or misclassify positions; sample from only 4 seasons may limit generalizability; injury severity and mechanism not captured; return-to-play timelines not analyzed; no biomechanical or training load data available.
practicalImplications: NFL strength and conditioning and sports medicine staff should prioritize hamstring injury prevention resources for linebackers and defensive backs, who demonstrate significantly elevated injury rates. Mid-season monitoring (weeks 7–10) should intensify given the observed temporal clustering. Playing surface and short-rest scheduling do not appear to be modifiable risk factors based on this data.
athleteDev: Position-specific hamstring training should be emphasized for developing LBs and DBs in football programs. The high sprint and change-of-direction demands of these positions place them at greatest risk, necessitating targeted eccentric strengthening, sprint mechanics work, and load monitoring throughout the season.
rtp: Hamstring injury return-to-play protocols in NFL players should consider position-specific demands; LBs and DBs face the highest recurrence risk given their injury incidence profile. Progressive sprinting programs and criteria-based functional testing are essential before return to full practice and game activity for these positions.
